title: DevTools Evidence Kit: Prove What the Browser Built
summary: Resolve a disputed browser fix by choosing evidence panels deliberately, testing a narrow hypothesis, repairing saved source, and verifying after reload.
objectives: Choose DevTools evidence that matches structure, style, accessibility, or network questions.
Distinguish a temporary runtime experiment from a persisted source repair.

steps.title: Close the debugging loop · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, what turns a successful temporary experiment into a verified repair?
steps.why: A deliberate panel choice turns browser inspection into evidence instead of random experimentation. Working through close the debugging loop narrows the temporary-fix dispute while you inspect the artifact.

content.title: Close the debugging loop — temporary-fix dispute
content.text: Commit to the claim that explains the temporary-fix dispute evidence without assigning work to the wrong layer.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute inspect, identify which devtools evidence boundary “Close the debugging loop” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Close the debugging loop” evidence during inspect.
For temporary-fix dispute inspect, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Close the debugging loop.”

options.text: Leave DevTools open so its temporary state remains visible.
Apply the responsible change to saved source, reload from that source, and repeat the failing check.
Capture a screenshot and skip the original reproduction steps.

stimulus.title: Temporary edit versus saved file · temporary-fix dispute
stimulus.caption: Evidence collected before and after reloading the page. Case: temporary-fix dispute; focus: close the debugging loop.

steps.title: Separate source and DOM · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, which statement accounts for the recovered tree?
steps.why: Source, the browser-built DOM, layout, and pixels are related stages rather than interchangeable evidence. Working through separate source and dom narrows the temporary-fix dispute as you connect the explanation to evidence.

content.text: A browser parses source into object models, applies error-recovery rules, derives accessibility information and styles, computes layout, and paints output. Source shows the input text; the DOM shows the current parsed structure; computed styles and layout show resolved presentation; pixels show only the final visual result. Apply this distinction during the temporary-fix dispute read task.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute read, identify which browser pipeline boundary “Separate source and DOM” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Separate source and DOM” evidence during read.
For temporary-fix dispute read, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Separate source and DOM.”

options.text: Source and DOM must remain identical character for character after parsing.
Source is parser input; the DOM is the browser’s current constructed representation and may include recovery.
The DOM is a screenshot made from the final pixels.

steps.title: Interpret a reload · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, why did the edited heading revert?
steps.why: A deliberate panel choice turns browser inspection into evidence instead of random experimentation. Working through interpret a reload narrows the temporary-fix dispute before the result is shown.

content.title: Interpret a reload — temporary-fix dispute
content.text: Commit to the claim that explains the temporary-fix dispute evidence without assigning work to the wrong layer.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute predict, identify which devtools evidence boundary “Interpret a reload” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Interpret a reload” evidence during predict.
For temporary-fix dispute predict, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Interpret a reload.”

options.text: The monitor cached the old pixels inside the display cable.
The Elements edit changed the current DOM experiment, not the saved HTML file.
HTML headings are forbidden from being saved after DevTools opens.

steps.title: Rebuild browser pipeline · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: Within the temporary-fix dispute, reconstruct the browser pipeline in causal order.
steps.why: Source, the browser-built DOM, layout, and pixels are related stages rather than interchangeable evidence. Ordering the temporary-fix dispute exposes its first unsupported causal handoff.

content.text: Do not order by vocabulary or visual position. Test whether each event creates the conditions required by the next event in the temporary-fix dispute.

hints: In the temporary-fix dispute sequence, locate the first event whose required input is not ready.
Separate temporary-fix dispute browser pipeline events from later visible outcomes.
Rebuild the temporary-fix dispute sequence one causal handoff at a time.

options.text: Document bytes arrive as source input
The parser tokenizes source and applies recovery rules
The browser constructs the current document tree
Style, layout, and paint produce visible pixels

steps.title: Inspect an accessible name · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, which evidence best verifies the current role and accessible name of a control?
steps.why: A deliberate panel choice turns browser inspection into evidence instead of random experimentation. Working through inspect an accessible name narrows the temporary-fix dispute while you defend the selected claim.

content.title: Inspect an accessible name — temporary-fix dispute
content.text: Commit to the claim that explains the temporary-fix dispute evidence without assigning work to the wrong layer.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute answer, identify which devtools evidence boundary “Inspect an accessible name” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Inspect an accessible name” evidence during answer.
For temporary-fix dispute answer, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Inspect an accessible name.”

options.text: Only the visible border color around the control.
The browser accessibility inspection for that control.
The Network response time for the HTML document.

steps.title: Locate painting · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, at which stage do resolved geometry and visual layers become displayable output?
steps.why: Source, the browser-built DOM, layout, and pixels are related stages rather than interchangeable evidence. Working through locate painting narrows the temporary-fix dispute while you inspect the artifact.

content.text: Commit to the claim that explains the temporary-fix dispute evidence without assigning work to the wrong layer.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute inspect, identify which browser pipeline boundary “Locate painting” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Locate painting” evidence during inspect.
For temporary-fix dispute inspect, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Locate painting.”

options.text: Before source bytes reach the parser.
After style and layout, during painting and compositing toward pixels.
When the operating system changes the file extension.

stimulus.title: Recovered document tree · temporary-fix dispute
stimulus.caption: The browser-built structure after parsing malformed source. Case: temporary-fix dispute; focus: locate painting.

lines.label: parser
lines.text: end tags inferred before document end

steps.title: Choose the right panel · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: In the temporary-fix dispute, a stylesheet request returns 404. Which panel provides the direct evidence?
steps.why: A deliberate panel choice turns browser inspection into evidence instead of random experimentation. Working through choose the right panel narrows the temporary-fix dispute before the result is shown.

content.title: Choose the right panel — temporary-fix dispute
content.text: Commit to the claim that explains the temporary-fix dispute evidence without assigning work to the wrong layer.

hints: During temporary-fix dispute predict, identify which devtools evidence boundary “Choose the right panel” asks about.
Compare every temporary-fix dispute option with the named “Choose the right panel” evidence during predict.
For temporary-fix dispute predict, reject claims that skip a required layer or invent unavailable evidence for “Choose the right panel.”

options.text: Elements, because DOM nodes generate every HTTP status.
Network, filtered to the stylesheet request and response status.
The color picker, because missing resources are color defects.

steps.title: Write the evidence note · temporary-fix dispute
steps.instruction: Write a concise case note for the temporary-fix dispute. Explain the mechanism, cite observed evidence, and reject one plausible alternative.
steps.why: Explaining the temporary-fix dispute from memory exposes gaps that selected answers can hide. Cite the mechanism and its evidence.

content.title: Transfer brief
content.text: A teammate must be able to reproduce your conclusion without seeing the earlier answer choices. Name evidence and limits, not only the final verdict.

hints: Start the temporary-fix dispute case note with the earliest observed event.
Use these temporary-fix dispute technical anchors where they fit: Elements, Network, saved source, source, DOM.
Close the temporary-fix dispute note by naming one alternative and the evidence that rules it out.
